metadata.category: Bit Manipulation | Sliding Window | Binary Search

content.problemStatement:
Given an array AAA having NNN elements, a subarray SSS of AAA is called `good` if XOR(S)≥KXOR(S) \geq KXOR(S)≥K, where XOR(S)XOR(S)XOR(S) denotes the bitwise XOR of all the elements of the subarray SSS.

Find the length of the **smallest** subarray SSS which is `good`. If there is no `good` subarray, print −1-1−1 instead.


Input Format

The first line of input contains a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases. The description of the TTT test cases follows.
The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and KKK - as described in the problem statement.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,...,ANA_1, A_2,..., A_NA1​,A2​,...,AN​, AiA_iAi​ representing the elements of the array AAA.


Output Format
For each test case, output in a single line, the length of the shortest subarray which is `good`. If there is no `good` subarray, print −1-1−1.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 200000
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains two integers N and K. The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1 … AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the length of the shortest subarray whose XOR is at least K, or -1 if no such subarray exists.

sampleTestCases.explanation: First test: the shortest good subarrays are of length 4 (positions 1‑4 or 2‑5) with XOR 15. Second test: element A4 = 8 already satisfies XOR >= 7, so answer is 1. Third test: no subarray reaches XOR 20, answer -1.

solution.approach: For a fixed length L we can check in O(N) whether any subarray of length L has XOR >= K using prefix XORs: xor(i,i+L‑1) = pref[i+L] ^ pref[i]. The predicate "exists a good subarray of length L" is monotone (if it holds for L it also holds for any larger length), therefore we can binary‑search the minimal L. For each test case we compute the prefix XOR array once, then binary‑search between 1 and N. The check for a given L scans all N‑L+1 windows and stops early when a satisfactory XOR is found. Overall complexity per test case is O(N log N) and memory O(N).
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log N) per test case (N is the size of the array).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for the prefix XOR array.
